entry.title: Re: Bank Statement Date in Closed Period
entry.content:
Hello!

In bank statement processing the posting date of the accounting documents is derived from the bank statement date: your statement date = 11.12.2025

A practical solution will be to change posting date during reprocessing.  When you reprocess bank statement items:

  1. Open Reprocess Bank Statement Items ->  select the line item ->  choose:  Edit / Change
  2.  Manually override posting date, change from 11.12.2025 to desired date.

The outcome will be document is posted in current open period, statement date stays historically correct and no need to reopen period.

 

Alternative option is to  use “value date / entry date” (if configured), some setups allow posting date derived from entry date or value date. If your bank provides these fields correctly you can map posting date away from statement date.

 

Important consideration when you post in current open period:

  • Financial posting date = current open period
  • Bank statement date = Dec 2025

entry.title: Re: Closing Journal Entries Are Missing in Generated e-Le...
entry.content:
Hello!

External document types for the e-Ledger are given by GIB and must be defined in the "Specify External Document Type" configuration activity, and then SAP document types must be mapped in the separate "Assign SAP Document Type to External Document Type" activity. Even if "ZK" is maintained at the FI level, if it is not explicitly mapped to a GIB external document type in the e-Ledger configuration, the report engine will silently ignore those journal entries when building the TXT file.

Specify External Document Type, confirm the GIB compliant external type for year-end closing exists. Assign SAP Document Type to External Document Type, confirm document type ZK is explicitly mapped here.

Another reason could be that the special/closing period is not included in the report run. Year-end closing entries in SAP are typically posted to special periods (period 13, 14, 15, or 16), not period 12. The e-Ledger run for December 2025 may only be selecting fiscal period 12 without including the special closing periods. When executing the report in Run Statutory Reports, verify whether the selection parameters include special periods. If your closing entries are posted with a special period, the report must be explicitly parameterized to capture them.

Since this is a productive system with a GIB legal deadline, you should simultaneously open an SAP support case.

entry.title: Re: Sales Doc type for Rev Accruals
entry.content:
Hello!

Using the configure standard order type activity in CBC,  you can create a new custom sales order type, for example ZRA (or any name that makes sense), by copying the standard OR template. This automatically brings across all configuration settings tied to OR so you are not starting from scratch.

Once deployed to the system, this new type behaves like a standard sales order type. Can be customised specifically for needed revenue accrual process, for example, making it non-billing relevant so the orders never appear in billing runs, assigning separate number ranges so they are easy to identify in reporting, and adjusting pricing or account determination if accrual postings follow a different logic than standard sales orders.

The end result is a clean separation between revenue accrual orders and billing orders, which directly eliminates the manual filtering work.

One thing to confirm before is whether the system is currently on SAP CBC or still on the older Manage Your Solution configuration tool. This determines whether the capability is available or requires a short migration step first.

Below is a useful link about create custom sales order types in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/create-custom-sales-order-types-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/14349066

entry.title: Re: insert only if not exist
entry.content:
There are a few ways to do what you want ...

We'll start by creating the table+index and performing an initial insert:

CREATE TABLE verify(name varchar(50), condition text, error text)
go
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ind_verify ON verify( name )
go

insert verify (name, condition, error)
values ('name1', 'good condition', 'good error')
go

For each approach we'll perform two inserts:

  1. one insert for the previous name (eg, 'name1') with 'bad condition' and 'bad error'; no insert should be performed
  2. one for the next name (eg, 'name2') with 'good condition' and 'good error'; an insert should be performed

insert / select / where not exists(...)

-- should NOT insert

insert verify (name, condition, error)
select 'name1', 'bad condition', 'bad error'
where not exists(select name from verify where name = 'name1')
go

-- should insert

insert verify (name, condition, error)
select 'name2', 'good condition', 'good error'
where not exists(select name from verify where name = 'name2')
go

if not exists(...) / insert

-- should NOT insert

if not exists(select name from verify where name = 'name2')
begin
        insert verify (name, condition, error)
        values ('name2', 'bad condition', 'bad error')
end
go

-- should insert

if not exists(select name from verify where name = 'name3')
begin
        insert verify (name, condition, error)
        values ('name3', 'good condition', 'good error')
end
go

merge / when not matched

-- should NOT insert

merge into verify as tgt
      using (select 'name3','bad condition', 'bad error') as src (name,condition,error)
      on tgt.name = src.name
      when not matched
      then insert (name,condition,error)
           values (src.name, src.condition, src.error)
go

-- should insert

merge into verify as tgt
      using (select 'name4','good condition', 'good error') as src (name,condition,error)
      on tgt.name = src.name
      when not matched
      then insert (name,condition,error)
           values (src.name, src.condition, src.error)
go

At this point we should have 4 rows in our table, all with 'good condition' and 'good error' entries [NOTE: output has been edited to remove excessive spacing]:

select *
from verify
order by name
go

 name  condition      error
 ----- -------------- ----------
 name1 good condition good error
 name2 good condition good error
 name3 good condition good error
 name4 good condition good error

Obviously these example are basic T-SQL syntax.  I can't speak to how easy (or hard) these are to implement if using a programmed front end (eg, python, perl,), let alone how to handle any special characters that may be embedded in the values of the two text fields.

entry.title: Re: 100558 - Map Format Data for Payments: User-Defined F...
entry.content:
Hello!

FPAYHX is the SAP internal structure called "Payment Medium: Prepared Data for Payment." It acts as a container that holds all payment related data before it is written into the output XML file by the DME (Data Medium Exchange) engine.

REF02 is a standard field within SAP Structure FPAYHX that stores user defined field information. The REF fields (REF01 through REF15) content is entirely format-specific, meaning SAP has decided what data goes into each REF slot differently for each payment format tree (CGI_CT, CH_CGI_XML_CT, DE_CGI_XML_CT, etc.).

REF fields (FPAYHX-REF01 through REF15) are format specific.  When FPAYHX-REF02 is used as the source for <BldgNb> in the CH_CGI_XML_CT tree, it does not mean REF02 equals building number, it means SAP has programmatically loaded the building number value into REF02 during event execution.

SAP Note 2513995 contains an Excel file that covers the mapping of all reference fields in the generic and country-specific CGI trees.

  • FPAYHX-REF02 means what data is loaded into it for the CH format (likely the creditor building number extracted from the vendor address)
  • FPAYHX-REF14 means what condition data is stored (likely a flag or indicator used to decide whether the structured or unstructured address format applies)

When you see something like FPAYHX-REF02+010(10) in the Excel or in the format tree, this means the REF02 field is a long string field and the data you want starts at offset 10 (character position 11) and is 10 characters long. Multiple different data values can be packed into a single REF field using different offsets. This is how SAP fits many pieces of information into the 15 REF slots available.

The fact that FPAYHX-REF14 appears in the condition tab (not the Source tab) for <BldgNb> means in the DME format tree, a condition controls whether a node is output at all. So REF14 is being used as a switch, it likely contains a flag value that indicates whether the creditor address is in structured format (with separate StrNm + BldgNb) or unstructured format (with AdrLine entries). The CGI format supports both structured address (with separate <StrtNm>, <BldgNb>, <PstCd>, <TwnNm>, <Ctry>) and unstructured address (with <AdrLine> entries).

On Public Cloud exist no no access to OBPM1/SE11, the relevant event logic is handled internally by SAP's standard class and its country-specific methods. Can’t be modifed in Public Cloud, the values are populated automatically by SAP from the vendor master address data when the payment run executes.

If <BldgNb> tag is not being populated in the output XML, the most likely reasons are:

  • The building number is not maintained separately in the vendor master address (it may be concatenated into the street name field instead)
  • The REF14 condition evaluates to false because the address format indicator is set to unstructured

entry.title: A Customer Perspective: Sequencing SuccessFactors “Latest” Experiences for Better Adoption
entry.content:
SuccessFactors continues to invest in modern user experiences—something customers generally support because it improves usability, accessibility, and long-term innovation. However, a pattern is emerging that many customers are now experiencing in real time: multiple “Latest” experiences are arriving in parallel, and the combined impact is creating unnecessary adoption risk.

Two major examples are happening around the same time for many organizations:

  • Latest Home Page experience
  • Latest Performance Form experience

To support adoption without compromising product direction, I propose adjusting the timelines for the impacted legacy performance form experience to give customers room to transition in a structured way.

Requested adjustments for Legacy Performance Form Experience:

  • End of Maintenance: move to May 2027
  • Deletion Date: move to November 2027

This provides two important benefits:

  1. Sequencing: customers can implement and stabilize the Latest Home Page first, then move to the Latest Performance Form experience based on their cycle timing.
  2. Change management quality: customers can run enablement properly—communications, training, pilot groups, updated job aids, and support readiness—rather than pushing two major UI changes into one compressed period.

    Seeing the same challenges? Add your vote to the Customer Influence request: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#idea/374034

entry.title: SAP Incentive Management - Introducing Data Explorer (SQL Query Viewer)
entry.content:
This is currently under Feature Flag. Kindly create a SAP Support Ticket for requesting the access for Data Explorer.
This feature is not available in Oracle and legacy HANA stack.

What is Data Explorer?

Data Explorer is a self-service SQL editor built directly into the application. It allows administrators to:

  • Run SELECT queries against TCMP and EXT schemas.
  • View results in an interactive data table.
  • Verify data loads quickly and efficiently.
  • Troubleshoot issues without relying on external database clients.
  • Perform on-demand analysis to support business decisions.

This means administrators can securely query incentive-related table data directly inside the platform, reducing dependency on IT teams or third-party tools or asking SAP Technical Support teams via tickets .

How It Works

  1. Secure Access – No VPN or external SQL client required.
  2. Built-in SQL Editor – Write and execute queries in a familiar interface.
  3. Interactive Results – Data tables allow filtering, sorting, and validation.
  4. Schema Support – Direct access to TCMP (transactional compensation data) and EXT (extended data sources).
  5. SELECT Statement - Only SELECT Statements are only allowed with Query Limits (Refer Help Documentation)

Why It’s Useful for Incentive Management

Incentive programs often involve complex calculations, multiple data sources, and frequent adjustments. Data Explorer helps streamline these processes:

  • Data Validation Quickly confirm whether incentive data loads (e.g., sales transactions, quotas, payouts) are accurate.
  • Troubleshooting Identify missing or inconsistent records that could delay compensation cycles.
  • Transparency Provide clear audit trails by querying incentive-related data directly.
  • Agility Run ad-hoc analysis to test new incentive structures or simulate payout scenarios.
  • Reduced IT Dependency Empower administrators to answer business questions without waiting for IT support.

Example Use Cases - Useful querying via Data Explorer

  • Verify Sales Transactions: Ensure all eligible deals are captured before payout.
  • Verify Plugin Query Functions : Ensure all the plugins are saved properly and calculates accurate
  • Check Quota Assignments: Confirm that quotas are loaded correctly for each rep.
  • Analyze Payout Trends: Run queries to identify anomalies in incentive distribution.
  • Audit Adjustments: Track manual overrides or corrections applied to incentive data.

 

Steps to enable Data Explorer

Go to User Administration - Select Role - Add Permissions for Data Explorer and Assign to User

From Home Page - Select Data Manager - Self Service from dropdown Menu

You will see left menu Data Explorer (only when Feature Flag is enabled) to query your table (TCMP or EXT Schema)

Using TCMP Schema - This is most useful table for debugging when Plugin Query Functions to be debugged

Using EXT Schema - Table Name must be remembered to query

 

Most Common errors you will see in Data Explorer

 

 

 

Conclusion

Data Explorer bridges the gap between technical administration and business agility. By enabling secure, in-app SQL queries, it empowers incentive management teams to validate, troubleshoot, and analyze data in real time — ensuring that compensation programs remain fair, accurate, and motivating.

entry.title: Re: Warehouse and Transportation Synchronization in SAP S/4HANA Using SAP EWM and TM
entry.content:
A minor correction to step 13: Posting goods movement in EWM updates only the LE side. TM is updated via the LDAP notification message triggered by either “Finish Loading” or “Departure from Checkpoint.” Note that “Finish Loading” is optional and requires the Early Update function. Therefore, “Departure from Checkpoint” is the final point at which EWM updates TM.
This clarification is important because many customers reported SAP cases and expected FU/FO updates in TM immediately after EWM posts goods movement to LE—assuming the update flows EWM → LE → TM. That is not the case. To avoid duplicate updates from both LE and EWM, once a TU is created in EWM and warehouse activities have started, TM accepts updates only from the EWM LDAP notification.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/f44d8a515b4b135ce10000000a445394.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

entry.title: Preparing Operational Context Before Opening an SAP HANA Support Incident
entry.content:
Introduction
SAP HANA environments may involve multiple technical layers, including database services, application components, operating systems, storage, networking, monitoring, and high-availability mechanisms.

When operational issues occur, the initially visible symptom does not always identify the originating technical layer responsible for the observed behavior. Effective investigation therefore often depends not only on the observed symptom itself, but also on the quality and structure of the operational context collected during the initial investigation phase.

SAP provides self-service resources through SAP Help Portal documentation, SAP Notes, SAP Knowledge Base Articles, SAP HANA cockpit, SAP for Me, Guided Answers, troubleshooting guides, and related support resources.

At the same time, many operational situations still require system-specific investigation by SAP Support.

 

This article summarizes a documentation-based preparation approach for SAP HANA incident handling using publicly available SAP troubleshooting concepts and support resources.

It does not provide troubleshooting instructions, root-cause conclusions, configuration recommendations, or performance tuning guidance.

Its purpose is to support clearer communication, reduce unnecessary clarification cycles, and help investigation activities focus more efficiently on the affected technical area.

Observable Symptoms and Technical Interpretation
During incident situations, operational discussions sometimes begin with broad conclusions such as:

  • “The database is down.”
  • “System Replication failed.”
  • “Performance is degraded.”
  • “Recovery is broken.”
  • “Monitoring indicates the database is healthy.”

However, SAP HANA landscapes may involve multiple interacting technical layers, including:

  • database runtime
  • application processing
  • client connectivity
  • monitoring infrastructure
  • operating-system components
  • storage and network infrastructure
  • landscape-management components

 

Observable behavior does not automatically identify the originating technical component responsible for the situation.

For this reason, SAP troubleshooting documentation typically emphasizes monitoring interpretation, timeframe alignment, trace analysis, and correlation of technical observations before conclusions are validated.

Operational preparation therefore begins with collecting observable facts rather than immediately assigning root-cause responsibility.

Initial Self-Service Investigation
During the initial investigation phase, publicly available SAP resources may help clarify whether the observed behavior matches documented operational scenarios.

Examples may include:

  • SAP for Me knowledge search
  • SAP Help Portal documentation
  • SAP Notes
  • SAP Knowledge Base Articles
  • SAP HANA cockpit monitoring information
  • SAP HANA alerts
  • SAP HANA system views documentation
  • SAP Guided Answers
  • SAP Support Log Assistant, where applicable

These resources may help identify:

  • documented operational behavior
  • configuration prerequisites
  • monitoring interpretation guidance
  • operational limitations
  • affected technical areas
  • related troubleshooting documentation

This initial investigation phase may help narrow the technical scope before SAP Support involvement begins.

Suggested Initial Preparation Flow

When preparing an SAP HANA support incident, a structured preparation approach may help organize the observed situation and support clearer communication during investigation.

The following examples summarize commonly relevant preparation areas based on publicly available SAP troubleshooting concepts.

1. Define the Affected Scope
Examples may include:

  • single tenant or complete SAP HANA system
  • productive or non-productive environment
  • specific application or multiple applications
  • single host or multiple hosts
  • isolated user impact or broader landscape impact

Defining the affected scope may help align the investigation with the observed operational behavior.

2. Identify the Relevant Timeframe
Examples may include:

  • exact timestamp of the first observation
  • whether the behavior is continuous or intermittent
  • duration of the observed behavior
  • whether the issue started after:
    • maintenance activity
    • restart activity
    • takeover activity
    • upgrade or patching activity
    • infrastructure or network changes

Timeframe alignment is frequently important for monitoring interpretation, trace analysis, and correlation of observable system behavior.

3. Collect Observable Monitoring Information
Depending on the scenario, relevant observations may include:

  • active SAP HANA alerts
  • service status information
  • resource utilization observations
  • workload-related observations
  • replication status information
  • backup-related observations
  • connectivity-related observations

SAP HANA cockpit, SAP HANA monitoring tools, and SAP HANA system views may support the collection of this information.

The purpose of collecting monitoring information is to document observable system behavior during the affected timeframe.

4. Collect Relevant Diagnosis Information
Depending on the affected technical area, SAP Support may request diagnosis information relevant to the observed timeframe and system scope.

Examples may include diagnosis bundles, logs, traces, or full-system-info-dumps as described in SAP documentation and SAP Knowledge Base Articles.

The relevance of diagnosis information depends on the technical scenario being investigated.

5. Document Already Performed Validation Steps
Examples may include:

  • restart attempts
  • connectivity validation
  • reproduction attempts
  • application-level observations
  • infrastructure-related observations
  • monitoring validation steps

Documenting already performed validation steps may help reduce ambiguity during investigation and support clearer communication between involved teams and SAP Support.

Monitoring and Evidence Collection
SAP HANA documentation describes monitoring and troubleshooting using alerts, monitoring tools, traces, diagnosis information, and related analysis resources.

Depending on the scenario, relevant observations may include:

  • SAP HANA alerts
  • service status information
  • workload-related observations
  • resource utilization indicators
  • replication status information
  • backup-related observations
  • connectivity-related observations

SAP HANA cockpit, monitoring interfaces, and SAP HANA diagnosis tools may support the collection and interpretation of this information.

Collected monitoring information may help provide additional technical context during investigation.

Diagnosis Files and Traces
SAP HANA troubleshooting documentation refers to diagnosis files, traces, logs, and related diagnostic tools as part of technical investigation.

Depending on the affected area, SAP Support may request diagnosis information relevant to the affected timeframe and technical scope.

SAP KBA 1732157 describes the collection of SAP HANA diagnosis information, including full-system-info-dumps.

 

The relevance of diagnosis information depends on the technical scenario being investigated.

Providing diagnosis information together with relevant operational context and timeframe details may help improve investigation alignment and interpretability during analysis.

Choosing the Appropriate SAP Component
The selected SAP support component should reflect the affected technical area as closely as possible.

Examples may include:

  • backup and recovery
  • system replication
  • performance
  • connectivity
  • SQL processing
  • security
  • installation and lifecycle management
  • cockpit and monitoring

Component selection should ideally reflect the observable technical area rather than an unvalidated root-cause assumption.

Working with SAP Support
SAP Support analysis is based on system-specific evidence collected from the affected environment.

Depending on the scenario, investigation activities may involve:

  • trace interpretation
  • monitoring correlation
  • workload analysis
  • topology evaluation
  • replication-state analysis
  • recovery validation
  • connectivity verification

Structured operational context may support clearer communication and investigation alignment during analysis.

Useful incident preparation may therefore include:

  • precise timestamps
  • reproducibility information
  • affected system scope
  • business impact
  • collected monitoring evidence
  • relevant diagnosis information
  • already performed validation steps


Conclusion
A structured operational context may help create a clearer shared understanding of the observed situation between involved teams and SAP Support.

SAP HANA investigations may require analysis across multiple technical layers, including database runtime, connectivity, infrastructure, monitoring, and application interaction.

Public SAP documentation and self-service resources may help identify documented operational behavior during the initial investigation phase.

When deeper analysis is required, organized operational context and relevant diagnosis information may support clearer communication, reduce unnecessary clarification cycles, and help investigation activities focus more efficiently on the affected technical area.

References

  • SAP for Me Knowledge Search
  • SAP HANA Troubleshooting and Performance Analysis Guide
  • SAP HANA Administration Guide for SAP HANA Platform
  • SAP HANA SQL and System Views Reference
  • SAP HANA Cockpit Documentation
  • SAP Guided Answers
  • SAP Support Log Assistant
  • SAP KBA 1732157 – Collecting diagnosis information for SAP HANA [VIDEO]


Disclaimer
This article summarizes publicly available SAP documentation and general troubleshooting concepts.

It does not replace SAP documentation, SAP Notes, SAP Knowledge Base Articles, or SAP Support procedures.

Only official SAP documentation and SAP Notes are authoritative.

entry.title: Exploring AI-Powered SAP Integrations using SAP Integration Suite and LLM APIs
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence is rapidly becoming part of modern enterprise architectures. As SAP Integration professionals, one interesting area to explore is how integration platforms can orchestrate AI-enabled workflows.

As part of my recent learning journey, I explored how SAP Integration Suite can integrate with Large Language Models (LLMs) and built a simple AI-powered Error Resolver demo using SAP Cloud Integration and Groq APIs.

Understanding the Basics

Before implementing the solution, it is important to understand a few key concepts.

What are LLMs?

Large Language Models (LLMs) are AI models trained on large amounts of textual data. Examples include GPT models, Llama models, Gemini, and Claude.

These models can:

  • Understand prompts
  • Generate responses
  • Summarize information
  • And assist with troubleshooting and analysis

What is RAG?

RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation.

In a RAG architecture:

  1. Relevant context is retrieved
  2. The context is added to the prompt
  3. The LLM generates a more contextual and intelligent response

This improves the quality and relevance of AI-generated answers.

What are Embeddings and Vector Databases?

Embeddings are numerical vector representations of text.

These embeddings are usually stored in Vector Databases such as:

  • SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine
  • Pinecone
  • Weaviate
  • And others

The Vector Database helps retrieve semantically similar information during AI processing.

For this demo, I intentionally simplified the retrieval process using Groovy-based logic instead of implementing a real Vector Database. This allowed me to focus first on orchestration and AI integration concepts.

Integration as an Orchestration Layer

One of the most interesting learnings from this exercise was understanding the role of SAP Integration Suite as an orchestration layer.

Even though:

  • The LLM runs externally
  • Embeddings may be generated externally
  • Retrieval may happen through a Vector Database

The orchestration still remains controlled by SAP Cloud Integration.

The integration flow can manage:

  • Prompt preparation
  • API communication
  • Contextual augmentation
  • Response parsing
  • Final response handling

This makes SAP Integration Suite highly relevant in future AI-enabled enterprise architectures.

Demo Use Case – AI-Powered Error Resolver

The demo use case was intentionally kept simple.

Input Example:
“Mapping failed: InvoiceID missing”

The iFlow:

  1. Receives the error
  2. Builds contextual information
  3. Sends the prompt to a Groq-hosted LLM model
  4. Receives an AI-generated response
  5. Returns the probable root cause and resolution

Technologies Used

The demo was implemented using:

  • SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP Cloud Integration (SAP CI)
  • Groovy Scripts
  • Groq APIs
  • Llama 3.3 70B model
  • POSTMAN for API validation

High-Level Architecture

SAP CI → Prompt Orchestration → Groq LLM API → AI Response Parsing → Final Resolution

At this stage, retrieval was simulated through Groovy logic instead of a real Vector Database.

Key Learnings from this Exercise

Some important learnings from this exploration:

  • AI integrations are fundamentally orchestration-driven.
  • Prompt quality significantly impacts AI responses.
  • Integration platforms can become powerful AI orchestration layers.
  • Understanding APIs and response structures is critical.
  • Starting simple helps in understanding AI architecture practically.

Demo Video

iFlow Design

 

Final Thoughts

This exploration was a great starting point in understanding how SAP Integration landscapes may evolve alongside Generative AI technologies.

There is still much more to learn about:

  • Embeddings
  • Vector databases
  • AI governance
  • SAP AI Core
  • Production-grade RAG architectures.

However, building small practical demos is an excellent way to understand how these technologies fit together in real enterprise scenarios.

The goal is not to become an overnight AI expert, but to continuously explore how modern AI capabilities can complement enterprise integration architectures.

entry.title: Re: Activation of duplicate Business Partner check using...
entry.content:
Dear @j35400__,

Yes, this validation is supported in Public Cloud

To activate it, enable the duplicate check for the relevant Brazil CPF/CNPJ tax number category in SSCUI 102415 Maintain Tax Number Categories. SAP identifies this SSCUI as the Public Cloud configuration activity for activating the tax number category duplication check

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2886187

To make the check blocking, maintain SSCUI 103601 Change Message Control for Duplicate Tax Numbers and set Application Area BUPA_TAX, Message 400 from information message to Error for online and batch processing

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2886241

One limitation should be highlighted

SAP documents that this blocking behavior works in Manage Business Partner Master Data, while in the GUI-style Maintain Business Partner app the error may appear but the duplicate tax number can still be saved. Therefore the recommended business process should use Manage Business Partner Master Data for this control

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3736950

Summary:

Activate the duplicate check in SSCUI 102415 and make message BUPA_TAX 400 blocking in SSCUI 103601. Use Manage Business Partner Master Data as the controlled app for preventing duplicate CPF/CNPJ creation

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: How to cleanup the bgrfc units
entry.content:
Hi Team,

You can follow the below to limit the size of the BGRFC_IUNIT_HIST table:

  • To delete entries from history table BGRFC_IUNIT_HIST use the deletion methods like e.g. DELETE_UNITS_I_MAX_BY_DEST of the CL_BGRFC_UNIT_HISTORY class from an own report
  • To cleanup the BGRFC_IUNIT_HIST table you can simply drop the table by doing a truncate operation. This can be done by calling function 'DB_TRUNCATE_TABLE' with the table name BGRFC_IUNIT_HIST.
    Only the contents of the table will be deleted, the structure will remain.

Refer more details in SAP Note 2689595 - bgRFC: report RS_BGRFC_DEL_UNIT_HISTORY cannot be used in background job

For any further assistance , You can create a case in the Component : BC-MID-RFC-BG

 

~Gaurav

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content:
Hi,

I have made some changes to my local implementation.

I added try-except error handling to ensure malformed events don't crash the pipeline and enabled persistent sessions to prevent data loss.

  • Runtime: Python 3.12.
  • Protocol: MQTT v3.1.1.
  • Connection: Connected to a local RabbitMQ broker (Docker)

The main challenge was library compatibility; I had to ensure the Python client used MQTT v3.1.1 to match RabbitMQ's persistence behavior (using clean_session=False instead of the MQTT v5 clean_start).

What happens if the consumer is offline?
In my setup, the events are automatically queued. By using a persistent Client ID and setting clean_session=False, RabbitMQ treats the consumer as a "durable" subscriber. Any events published during downtime are stored in a backing queue and delivered the moment the consumer reconnects.

What happens if a malformed event is received?
The code uses a try-except block. If a malformed event arrives, it logs the JSON error and continues listening.

When a malformed (non-JSON) message is received, the consumer logs the error but remains active. When a valid message arrives, it is parsed and processed immediately.

The following log shows the consumer being restarted and immediately receiving a "Queued Event" that was published while it was offline.

Full log 

ChromaDB contents

Kr,
Joery

entry.title: Re: 生产工单组件API接口状态字段
entry.content:
Dear @wangking,

Based on my understanding the question refers to whether the status of a production order component line item can be retrieved via a released interface if not please clarify the question in English. Thank you

In Pubic Cloud the production order component line item can be read through released interfaces but there is no single generic component status API field that represents all statuses exactly as displayed in the production order UI

For the deletion-related component status, such as DLT the relevant released interface is Production Order (Version 2), technical service API_PRODUCTION_ORDER_2_SRV. The component entity A_ProductionOrderComponent_4 contains the field MatlCompIsMarkedForDeletion, which SAP describes as Indicator Material component is marked for deletion. This field should be used to determine whether a production order component line item is marked for deletion

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d35113ee62644d3abee1aaec148291d9/1b599045b0664da89f7728055e75f81c.html

For CDS/reporting-based consumption, SAP also provides manufacturing order status VDM objects, including the Manufacturing Order with Status area, for retrieving manufacturing order data together with system status information. For component-level status reporting the relevant released component/status CDS object should be used where the required status is exposed

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/0b426c3ae37e47aea2718312ea2f32ec.html

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/how-to-get-quot-dlt-quot-status-of-production-order-component/qaq-p/13644933

Therefore the requirement is supported where the required component status is available as a released component-level API/CDS field. For DLT/deletion status the correct standard API field is API_PRODUCTION_ORDER_2_SRV -> A_ProductionOrderComponent_4 -> MatlCompIsMarkedForDeletion. For any other component status the exact status name should first be confirmed and then mapped to the corresponding released API/CDS field

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Document and Reporting Compliance Cloud Edition: A New Generation of Global Tax Compliance
entry.content:
Hi Kris.

Yes, customers can now choose between the embedded and cloud options for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, depending on their business and compliance requirements.

However, the availability still depends on the country-specific localization and supported scope. While SAP has expanded the coverage and flexibility of both deployment options, there are still certain countries or scenarios that may only be supported through DRC cloud edition or embedded DRC.

Therefore, the deployment model should be validated based on the specific country requirements and supported localization scope.

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content:
Thanks for the challenge.

Protocol used: AMQP 1.0 (via SAP Event Mesh / Solace PubSub+).
Messages were consumed from a durable queue (BP_QUEUE) using the broker’s built-in client (Try Me ).

entry.title: Re: Introduction to Next-Gen SAP Ariba
entry.content:
The Autonomous Enterprise and Joule Work

At Sapphire, on 12th May 2026, SAP introduced the Autonomous Enterprise so humans and AI can work together to meet the accelerating demands of global business profitably, strategically and safely.
The company also introduced a unified SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) and deepened partnerships with Anthropic, Amazon Web Services (AWS), Google Cloud, Microsoft, NVIDIA and Palantir Technologies, among others.

  • News - SAP Unveils the Autonomous Enterprise
  • Training - Introducing Autonomous Enterprise

The Autonomous Enterprise is comprised of Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite and the SAP Business AI Platform and includes industry scenarios that combine AI innovation with the deep process and vertical expertise SAP is known for.

Joule Work is the user engagement component of the Joule solution. Joule Work will allow users to express in natural language what they want to accomplish, triggering Joule Assistants to coordinate teams of Joule Agents that will surface the right insights and automate routine work across business domains and systems to achieve the goal.

SAP Sapphire Innovation News Guide 2026 highlights product innovation that advances SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise. Section 1 talks about 'Joule Work'. Section 2 is about 'Autonomous Suite'.

Section 2.2 (Spend Management) summarizes the key innovations across Next-gen SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass in line with the vision of Autonomous Enterprise.
For more details on Autonomous Spend, kindly refer this post - Enabling Autonomous Spend Management with AI and Connected Processes

Joule Work is the user engagement component of the Joule solution. Joule Work will allow users to express in natural language what they want to accomplish, triggering Joule Assistants to coordinate teams of Joule Agents that will surface the right insights and automate routine work across business domains and systems to achieve the goal.

SAP Sapphire Innovation News Guide 2026 highlights product innovation that advances SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise. Section 1 talks about 'Joule Work'. Section 2 is about 'Autonomous Suite'.

Section 2.2 (Spend Management) summarizes the key innovations across Next-gen SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass in line with the vision of Autonomous Enterprise.
For more details on Autonomous Spend, kindly refer this post - Enabling Autonomous Spend Management with AI and Connected Processes

entry.title: Re: Partner TDD Onboarding for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition – Guide for Common Issues
entry.content:
The Autonomous Enterprise and Joule Work

At Sapphire, on 12th May 2026, SAP introduced the Autonomous Enterprise so humans and AI can work together to meet the accelerating demands of global business profitably, strategically and safely.
The company also introduced a unified SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) and deepened partnerships with Anthropic, Amazon Web Services (AWS), Google Cloud, Microsoft, NVIDIA and Palantir Technologies, among others.

  • News - SAP Unveils the Autonomous Enterprise
  • Training - Introducing Autonomous Enterprise

The Autonomous Enterprise is comprised of Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite and the SAP Business AI Platform and includes industry scenarios that combine AI innovation with the deep process and vertical expertise SAP is known for.

Joule Work is the user engagement component of the Joule solution. Joule Work will allow users to express in natural language what they want to accomplish, triggering Joule Assistants to coordinate teams of Joule Agents that will surface the right insights and automate routine work across business domains and systems to achieve the goal.

SAP Sapphire Innovation News Guide 2026 highlights product innovation that advances SAP’s vision of the Autonomous Enterprise. Section 1 talks about 'Joule Work'. Section 2 is about 'Autonomous Suite'.

Section 2.2 (Spend Management) summarizes the key innovations across Next-gen SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass in line with the vision of Autonomous Enterprise.

entry.title: Re: Embracing Sustainability with SAP Ariba
entry.content:
Upcoming Session: Get Ready for EUDR Compliance — Practical Guidance Starts Here
Thursday, May 28 | 6:30 PM - 7:30 PM IST | 03:00-04:00 p.m. CEST | 09:00-10:00 a.m. EDT | Register |

With updated EU guidance now published and EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) implementation timelines confirmed (EUDR enforcement begins in December 2026), customer conversations are increasingly shifting from regulatory interpretation toward operational readiness and execution. The core challenge for customers remains unchanged: companies still need trusted traceability, due diligence and audit-ready compliance processes across increasingly complex global supply chains.

This webinar with SAP and LiveEO experts will provide practical guidance on preparing for EUDR compliance, covering the latest EU updates, SAP Green Token traceability and DDS workflows and deforestation-risk intelligence with LiveEO's TradeAware.

entry.title: SAP Charlotte Innovation Showcase 2026 — Cross‑Industry SAP Lightning Talks
entry.content:
SAP Community Charlotte is excited to host the SAP Charlotte Innovation Showcase 2026, a virtual event featuring 15‑minute lightning talks from SAP professionals across industries.

The goal is to highlight innovative SAP solutions, SAP BTP architectures, AI‑enabled use cases, and real‑world transformation stories.

Event Curators:

  1. Atul Joshi — Curator & Chief Judge 1
  2. Aditya Chinni — Co‑Curator & Chief Judge 2

Format:

  1. 10 speakers max. (FIFO). 
  2. 15‑minute talk + 5‑minute Q&A
  3. Cross‑industry SAP topics (BTP, S/4HANA, AI, EDA, Integration, Data Sphere,  SAC, Industry Solutions)
  4. 5‑member expert judging panel
  5. Top 3 speakers receive the SAP Charlotte Innovator Award + Certificate

Call for Speakers:

We invite SAP architects, consultants, developers, and industry experts to submit proposals showcasing their SAP innovations.

Submission Requirements:

  1. Session title
  2. 3–4 sentence abstract
  3. Speaker bio
  4. SAP technologies used
  5. Industry focus

Certificates:
All presenters will receive an official Certificate of Participation issued by the SAP Community Charlotte Curators.

Join us to celebrate innovation, share knowledge, and strengthen the SAP community in Charlotte.

entry.title: Re: How to properly logout from a CAP mocked authentication?
entry.content:
With mocked authentication, HTTP BASIC is the default authentication mechanism. There are no cookies.  The cookies you see when using BAS are for connecting to BAS via the exposed port.  If you use approuter cookies are used by it. 

Browsers cache HTTP BASIC credentials. You can override the cached credentials by providing username in the url.  The username for basic authentication is provided in the url as in the example: https://username@host:port/path..   or https://username:password@host:port/path..

Note: URL encode username and password.
